result.markdown:
--- title: "Lab 2: Spatial Analysis and Visualization" subtitle: "Healthcare Access and Equity in Pennsylvania" author: "Yusuf Khalil" date: today format: html: code-fold: false toc: true toc-location: left theme: cosmo embed-resources: true execute: warning: false message: false --- ## Assignment Overview --- ## Part 1: Healthcare Access for Vulnerable Populations ### Research Question **Which Pennsylvania counties have the highest proportion of vulnerable populations (elderly + low-income) living far from hospitals?** Your analysis should identify counties that should be priorities for healthcare investment and policy intervention. ### Required Analysis Steps Complete the following analysis, documenting each step with code and brief explanations: #### Step 1: Data Collection (5 points) Load the required spatial data: - Pennsylvania county boundaries - Pennsylvania hospitals (from lecture data) - Pennsylvania census tracts **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(sf) library(tidyverse) library(tigris) library(tidycensus) library(scales) library(patchwork) library(here) # Load spatial data pa_counties <- st_read(here("data/Pennsylvania_County_Boundaries.shp")) districts <- st_read(here("data/districts.geojson")) hospitals <- st_read(here("data/hospitals.geojson")) census_tracts <- tracts(state = "PA", cb = TRUE) metro_areas <- core_based_statistical_areas(cb = TRUE) # Check that all data loaded correctly st_crs(hospitals) head(hospitals) count(hospitals) distinct(hospitals) count(census_tracts) st_crs(census_tracts) st_crs(pa_counties) ``` ::: **Questions to answer:** - How many hospitals are in your dataset? 222 - How many census tracts? 3445 - What coordinate reference system is each dataset in? Hospitals: WGS 84, Census Tracts: NAD83, Counties: WGS 84 / Pseudo-Mercator --- #### Step 2: Get Demographic Data Use `tidycensus` to download tract-level demographic data for Pennsylvania. **Required variables:** - Total population - Median household income - Population 65 years and over (you may need to sum multiple age categories) **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Get demographic data from ACS acs_vars_2023 <- load_variables(2023, "acs5", cache = TRUE) old_people <- c("B01001A_014", "B01001A_015", "B01001A_016", "B01001A_029", "B01001A_030", "B01001A_031", "B01001B_014", "B01001B_015", "B01001B_016", "B01001B_029", "B01001B_030", "B01001B_031", "B01001D_014", "B01001D_015", "B01001D_016", "B01001D_029", "B01001D_030", "B01001D_031") demp_data <- get_acs(geography = "Tract", variables = c(Total_population ="B01003_001", Median_household_income = "B19013_001" ), state = "PA", year = 2023, survey = "acs5", output = "wide" ) demo_data1 <- get_acs(geography = "Tract", variables = old_people, state = "PA", year = 2023, survey = "acs5", )%>% group_by(GEOID, NAME) %>% summarise(pop_65up = sum(estimate))%>% ungroup() #Does not work when output is wide demo_data_total <- inner_join(demp_data, demo_data1) view(demo_data_total) # Join to tract boundaries demo_data_final <- census_tracts %>% inner_join(demo_data_total, by = "GEOID") missing_income <- demo_data_final %>% filter(is.na(Median_household_incomeE)) missing_income median_income <- demo_data_final %>% summarise(median_income = median(Median_household_incomeE, na.rm = TRUE)) ``` ::: **Questions to answer:** - What year of ACS data are you using? 2023 - How many tracts have missing income data? 65 - What is the median income across all PA census tracts? $72,943.5 --- #### Step 3: Define Vulnerable Populations Identify census tracts with vulnerable populations based on TWO criteria: 1. Low median household income (choose an appropriate threshold) 2. Significant elderly population (choose an appropriate threshold) **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Filter for vulnerable tracts based on your criteria hist(demo_data_final$Median_household_incomeE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lab_2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} quantile(demo_data_final$Median_household_incomeE, na.rm = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 0% 25% 50% 75% 100% 13307.00 57863.50 72943.50 96690.75 250001.00 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} hist(demo_data_final$pop_65up) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lab_2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-2.png){width=672} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} quantile(demo_data_final$pop_65up) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 0% 25% 50% 75% 100% 0 406 643 918 2513 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} vulnerable_tracts <- demo_data_final %>% mutate( vuln_income = case_when( Median_household_incomeE <= 57863 ~ "vuln_income"), vuln_age = case_when( pop_65up >= 918 ~ "vuln_age" ) ) total_vuln_tracts <- vulnerable_tracts %>% filter(vuln_income == vuln_income | vuln_age == vuln_age) ``` ::: **Questions to answer:** - What income threshold did you choose and why? $57,863 — the 25th percentile of median household income across PA tracts, identified using the histogram and quantile function. Tracts below this threshold represent the lowest-earning quarter of the state. - What elderly population threshold did you choose and why? 918 — the 75th percentile of population aged 65+, meaning tracts above this have a disproportionately large elderly population compared to most PA tracts. - How many tracts meet your vulnerability criteria? 1,625 - What percentage of PA census tracts are considered vulnerable by your definition? 47% --- #### Step 4: Calculate Distance to Hospitals For each vulnerable tract, calculate the distance to the nearest hospital. **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Transform to appropriate projected CRS vulnerable_tracts_proj <- vulnerable_tracts %>% filter(vuln_income == "vuln_income" | vuln_age == "vuln_age") %>% st_transform(crs = 2272) hospitals_proj <- hospitals %>% st_transform(crs = 2272) # Calculate distance from each tract centroid to nearest hospital #Turn Vulnerable Tract centroids into points vuln_tract_centroids <- vulnerable_tracts_proj %>% st_centroid() #Finding distance to hosptial matrix dist_to_hosp <- st_distance(vuln_tract_centroids, hospitals_proj) apply(dist_to_hosp, 1, min) dist_to_hosp #Changing from feet to miles and rouding to 2 D.P vuln_tract_centroids$nearest_hospital_miles <- round(apply(dist_to_hosp, 1, min) / 5280 ,2) avg_dist_hosp <- mean(vuln_tract_centroids$nearest_hospital_miles) avg_dist_hosp view(vuln_tract_centroids) ``` ::: **Requirements:** - Use an appropriate projected coordinate system for Pennsylvania - Calculate distances in miles - Explain why you chose your projection **Questions to answer:** - What is the average distance to the nearest hospital for vulnerable tracts? 3.93 - What is the maximum distance? 19.16 - How many vulnerable tracts are more than 15 miles from the nearest hospital? 39 --- #### Step 5: Identify Underserved Areas Define "underserved" as vulnerable tracts that are more than 15 miles from the nearest hospital. **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create underserved variable underserved_tract <- vuln_tract_centroids %>% mutate( underserved = case_when( nearest_hospital_miles > 15 ~ "Underserved" ) ) underserved_tract %>% filter(underserved == "Underserved") %>% nrow() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 39 ``` ::: ::: **Questions to answer:** - How many tracts are underserved? 39 - What percentage of vulnerable tracts are underserved? 2.4% - Does this surprise you? Why or why not? No. Pennsylvania is a large state with both dense urban areas and sparsely populated rural regions. Most hospitals are concentrated in cities and suburban centers, so it's expected that a small number of rural tracts with vulnerable populations fall outside a 15-mile service radius. --- #### Step 6: Aggregate to County Level Use spatial joins and aggregation to calculate county-level statistics about vulnerable populations and hospital access. **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Spatial join tracts to counties pa_counties_proj <- pa_counties %>% st_transform(crs = 2272) underserved_tract_join <- st_join(underserved_tract, pa_counties_proj) # Aggregate statistics by county county_stats <- underserved_tract_join %>% st_drop_geometry(.) %>% group_by(COUNTY_NAM) %>% summarise( vulnerable_tracts = n(), underserved_tracts = sum(underserved == "Underserved", na.rm = TRUE) ) #County-level Statistics Calculations county_stats_summary <- sum(county_stats$vulnerable_tracts) avg_dist_nrst_hosp <- mean(underserved_tract$nearest_hospital_miles) total_vuln_pop <- sum(underserved_tract$Total_populationE) total_underserved <- sum(county_stats$underserved_tracts) underserved_tract %>% filter(underserved == "Underserved") %>% group_by(NAMELSADCO) %>% summarise(total_vuln_pop = sum(Total_populationE, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% arrange(desc(total_vuln_pop)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Simple feature collection with 24 features and 2 fields Geometry type: GEOMETRY Dimension: XY Bounding box: xmin: 1230745 ymin: 154142.5 xmax: 2752811 ymax: 954516.6 Projected CRS: NAD83 / Pennsylvania South (ftUS) # A tibble: 24 × 3 NAMELSADCO total_vuln_pop geometry 1 Clearfield County 29522 MULTIPOINT ((1761643 552819.8), (1771676 60… 2 Snyder County 11506 MULTIPOINT ((2146031 512151.8), (2156983 53… 3 Chester County 11417 MULTIPOINT ((2465898 169818.4), (2495360 18… 4 Bradford County 10201 MULTIPOINT ((2337737 885991.1), (2346226 83… 5 Pike County 8286 MULTIPOINT ((2712124 734907.2), (2752811 73… 6 Monroe County 7143 MULTIPOINT ((2596964 659216.4), (2624230 69… 7 Crawford County 6986 MULTIPOINT ((1230745 872213.6), (1251729 90… 8 Forest County 6785 MULTIPOINT ((1526711 801611.7), (1599178 79… 9 York County 5669 POINT (2367500 154142.5) 10 Cumberland County 5348 POINT (2109310 279890.1) # ℹ 14 more rows ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} #Visualisation for sanity hospitals_final <- st_join(hospitals_proj, vulnerable_tracts_proj, left = FALSE) underserved_tract_only <- vulnerable_tracts_proj %>% filter(GEOID %in% underserved_tract$GEOID[underserved_tract$underserved == "Underserved"]) hospital_buffer <- hospitals_final %>% st_buffer(dist = 79200) ggplot() + geom_sf(data = pa_counties, fill = "white", color = "gray") + geom_sf(data = hospital_buffer, fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.4) + geom_sf(data = hospitals, color = "red", size = 2) + geom_sf(data = underserved_tract_only, fill = "pink", colour = "gray" ) + theme_void() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lab_2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Required county-level statistics:** - Number of vulnerable tracts: 1625 - Number of underserved tracts: 39 - Percentage of vulnerable tracts that are underserved: 4/85 - Average distance to nearest hospital for vulnerable tracts: 3.96 - Total vulnerable population: 434055 **Questions to answer:** - Which 5 counties have the highest percentage of underserved vulnerable tracts? Cameron County, Forest County, Sullivan County, Pike County, Potter County - Which counties have the most vulnerable people living far from hospitals? Clearfield County, Snyder County, Chester County - Are there any patterns in where underserved counties are located? Underserved counties are predominantly located in rural, northern and central Pennsylvania, areas with low population density, limited infrastructure, and greater distances between towns --- #### Step 7: Create Summary Table Create a professional table showing the top 10 priority counties for healthcare investment. **Your Task:** ```{.r .cell-code} library(kableExtra) # Create and format priority counties table underserved_tract_descend <- underserved_tract %>% arrange(desc(nearest_hospital_miles)) library(kableExtra) underserved_tract_descend <- underserved_tract %>% st_drop_geometry() %>% arrange(desc(nearest_hospital_miles)) %>% head(10) %>% select(GEOID, NAMELSADCO, NAMELSAD, Total_populationE ,nearest_hospital_miles, underserved) underserved_tract_descend %>% kable( format = "html", col.names = c("Tract GEOID", "County","Tract", "Total Population","Distance (mi)", "Status"), caption = "Top 10 Priority Counties for Healthcare Investment", digits = 2, align = "llcl" ) %>% kable_styling( bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE, position = "center" ) %>% row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#2c3e50", color = "white") %>% column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>% footnote(general = "Source: ACS 2023. Underserved = nearest hospital > 15 miles.") ``` `````{=html}
Top 10 Priority Counties for Healthcare Investment
Tract GEOID County Tract Total Population Distance (mi) Status
42103950606 Pike County Census Tract 9506.06 6381 25.96 Underserved
42033330700 Clearfield County Census Tract 3307 2103 25.62 Underserved
42015951200 Bradford County Census Tract 9512 4852 24.81 Underserved
42029308000 Chester County Census Tract 3080 5763 22.19 Underserved
42033331000 Clearfield County Census Tract 3310 4814 21.89 Underserved
42033330800 Clearfield County Census Tract 3308 6843 21.40 Underserved
42103950503 Pike County Census Tract 9505.03 1905 21.20 Underserved
42133024001 York County Census Tract 240.01 5669 20.68 Underserved
42109070400 Snyder County Census Tract 704 5585 20.39 Underserved
42097082300 Northumberland County Census Tract 823 4953 20.03 Underserved
Note:
Source: ACS 2023. Underserved = nearest hospital > 15 miles.
````` **Requirements:** - Use `knitr::kable()` or similar for formatting - Include descriptive column names - Format numbers appropriately (commas for population, percentages, etc.) - Add an informative caption - Sort by priority (you decide the metric) --- ## Part 2: Comprehensive Visualization Using the skills from Week 3 (Data Visualization), create publication-quality maps and charts. ### Map 1: County-Level Choropleth Create a choropleth map showing healthcare access challenges at the county level. **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create county-level access map #Joining county level CT data to vulnerable tract data underserved_tract <- st_drop_geometry(underserved_tract) perc_vuln_trat <- left_join(demo_data_final, underserved_tract) #Summarising number of CT per County #Add filter so we only count number of VULNERABLE tracts CT_per_County <- perc_vuln_trat %>% filter(vuln_income == "vuln_income" | vuln_age == "vuln_age") %>% group_by(NAMELSADCO) %>% summarise( Number_of_ct = n() ) CT_per_County ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Simple feature collection with 67 features and 2 fields Geometry type: GEOMETRY Dimension: XY Bounding box: xmin: -80.51989 ymin: 39.7198 xmax: -74.77683 ymax: 42.18103 Geodetic CRS: NAD83 # A tibble: 67 × 3 NAMELSADCO Number_of_ct geometry 1 Adams County 9 MULTIPOLYGON (((-77.03122 39.80224, -77.02885 … 2 Allegheny County 180 MULTIPOLYGON (((-79.78506 40.40042, -79.77796 … 3 Armstrong County 9 MULTIPOLYGON (((-79.56745 40.57715, -79.56645 … 4 Beaver County 23 MULTIPOLYGON (((-80.26287 40.62011, -80.26001 … 5 Bedford County 8 POLYGON ((-78.65826 39.72283, -78.6338 39.7227… 6 Berks County 45 MULTIPOLYGON (((-75.72295 40.48971, -75.7067 4… 7 Blair County 20 MULTIPOLYGON (((-78.25984 40.66797, -78.26151 … 8 Bradford County 9 MULTIPOLYGON (((-76.78679 41.713, -76.82072 41… 9 Bucks County 58 MULTIPOLYGON (((-74.86049 40.12454, -74.85408 … 10 Butler County 22 MULTIPOLYGON (((-79.95176 40.92771, -79.94769 … # ℹ 57 more rows ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} vuln_tract_per_county <- perc_vuln_trat %>% filter(underserved == "Underserved") %>% group_by(NAMELSADCO) %>% summarise( number_of_ct = n() ) vuln_tract_per_county ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Simple feature collection with 24 features and 2 fields Geometry type: GEOMETRY Dimension: XY Bounding box: xmin: -80.51942 ymin: 39.72088 xmax: -74.79199 ymax: 42.00007 Geodetic CRS: NAD83 # A tibble: 24 × 3 NAMELSADCO number_of_ct geometry 1 Bradford County 2 POLYGON ((-76.53771 41.67941, -76.49344 41.60… 2 Cameron County 1 POLYGON ((-78.24858 41.50342, -78.24099 41.50… 3 Centre County 1 POLYGON ((-78.2272 40.89737, -78.22422 40.895… 4 Chester County 2 MULTIPOLYGON (((-75.90702 39.80072, -75.90542… 5 Clearfield County 8 POLYGON ((-78.63471 40.84263, -78.62212 40.83… 6 Crawford County 2 POLYGON ((-80.51942 41.67123, -80.51936 41.66… 7 Cumberland County 1 POLYGON ((-77.31678 40.16105, -77.30114 40.12… 8 Dauphin County 1 POLYGON ((-76.99299 40.56403, -76.99194 40.56… 9 Erie County 1 POLYGON ((-80.37484 41.8908, -80.37481 41.883… 10 Fayette County 1 POLYGON ((-79.58387 39.88562, -79.57918 39.87… # ℹ 14 more rows ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} #Caclualting percentage of vulnerable tracts underserved CT_per_County <- st_drop_geometry(CT_per_County) vuln_tract_per_county <- st_drop_geometry(vuln_tract_per_county) perc_vuln_trat <- vuln_tract_per_county %>% left_join(CT_per_County, by = "NAMELSADCO") %>% mutate(perc_counties_underserved = round((number_of_ct / Number_of_ct)*100, 2)) perc_vuln_trat ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 24 × 4 NAMELSADCO number_of_ct Number_of_ct perc_counties_underserved 1 Bradford County 2 9 22.2 2 Cameron County 1 1 100 3 Centre County 1 19 5.26 4 Chester County 2 32 6.25 5 Clearfield County 8 18 44.4 6 Crawford County 2 14 14.3 7 Cumberland County 1 28 3.57 8 Dauphin County 1 36 2.78 9 Erie County 1 51 1.96 10 Fayette County 1 23 4.35 # ℹ 14 more rows ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} #Gives pa counties with desirable geometry the correct county name so we can merge county_choropleth <- pa_counties %>% mutate(NAMELSADCO = str_to_title(COUNTY_NAM), NAMELSADCO = paste(NAMELSADCO, "County")) %>% left_join(perc_vuln_trat, by = "NAMELSADCO") #Mapping perc_vuln_tract somehow? ggplot() + geom_sf(data = pa_counties, fill = "white", color = "gray") + geom_sf(data = county_choropleth, aes(fill = perc_counties_underserved), colour = "white") + scale_fill_viridis_c( name = "% Vulnerable Tract", option = "magma" ) + geom_sf(data = hospitals, color = "red", size = 1) + labs( title = "Percentage of Vulnerable Tracts Underserved by County", subtitle = "Red points = Hospital locations", caption = "Source: ACS 2023, PA Hospital Data. Underserved = >15 miles from nearest hospital." ) + theme_void() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lab_2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Requirements:** - Fill counties by percentage of vulnerable tracts that are underserved: yes - Include hospital locations as points: yes - Use an appropriate color scheme: - Include clear title, subtitle, and caption - Use `theme_void()` or similar clean theme - Add a legend with formatted labels --- ### Map 2: Detailed Vulnerability Map Create a map highlighting underserved vulnerable tracts. **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create detailed tract-level map ggplot() + geom_sf(data = pa_counties, fill = "white", color = "gray") + geom_sf(data = hospitals, color = "red", size = 2) + geom_sf(data = underserved_tract_only, fill = "pink", colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5 ) + labs( title = "Underserved Vulnerable Census Tracts in Pennsylvania", subtitle = "Red points = Hospitals, Pink tracts = Vulnerable and >15 miles from nearest hospital", caption = "Source: ACS 2023, PA Hospital Data" ) + theme_void() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lab_2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Requirements:** - Show underserved vulnerable tracts in a contrasting color: Yes - Include county boundaries for context: yes - Show hospital locations: yes - Use appropriate visual hierarchy (what should stand out?): yes - Include informative title and subtitle: tbd --- ### Chart: Distribution Analysis Create a visualization showing the distribution of distances to hospitals for vulnerable populations. **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create distribution visualization scatter_plot <- underserved_tract %>% filter(vuln_income == "vuln_income" | vuln_age == "vuln_age") ggplot(scatter_plot, aes(x = nearest_hospital_miles, y = Total_populationE)) + geom_point() + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red")+ labs(x = "Distance", y = "Vulnerable Population Size") + theme_minimal() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lab_2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: -The scatter plot shows a weak positive relationship between distance to the nearest hospital and tract population size, with most vulnerable tracts clustered within 5 miles of a hospital. The nearly flat trend line suggests that distance to hospitals does not vary significantly with population size, though the sparse scatter beyond 15 miles highlights a small number of isolated tracts that may warrant targeted intervention. --- ## Part 3: Bring Your Own Data Analysis Choose your own additional spatial dataset and conduct a supplementary analysis. ### Challenge Options Choose ONE of the following challenge exercises, or propose your own research question using OpenDataPhilly data (https://opendataphilly.org/datasets/). --- ### Your Analysis **Your Task:** 1. **Find and load additional data** - Document your data source - Check and standardize the CRS - Provide basic summary statistics ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(sf) library(tidyverse) library(tigris) library(tidycensus) library(scales) library(patchwork) library(here) #Load datasets transit_stops <- st_read(here("data/Transit_Stops_(Spring_2025).shp")) st_crs(transit_stops) census_tracts2 <- tracts(state = "PA", cb = TRUE) rail_lines <- c("MFL", "BSL", "NHSL") trolley_lines <- c("10", "11", "13", "15", "34", "36", "101", "102") septa_stops <- transit_stops %>% mutate(mode = case_when( LineAbbr %in% rail_lines ~ "Rail", LineAbbr %in% trolley_lines ~ "Trolley", TRUE ~ "Bus" )) philly_tracts <- get_acs( geography = "tract", state = "PA", county = "Philadelphia", variables = c( total_pop = "B01003_001", white = "B02001_002", black = "B02001_003", asian = "B02001_005", hispanic = "B03003_003" ), year = 2022, geometry = TRUE, output = "wide" ) septa_stops <- st_transform(septa_stops, st_crs(philly_tracts)) #summary statistics stops_by_tract <- st_join(septa_stops, philly_tracts) stops_count <- stops_by_tract %>% st_drop_geometry() %>% group_by(NAME) %>% summarise(num_stops = n()) stops_count ``` ::: **Questions to answer:** - What dataset did you choose and why? SEPTA transit stop locations across Philadelphia. Public transit access is a key determinant of economic opportunity and quality of life, particularly for low-income and minority communities who are more likely to depend on public transport. - What is the data source and date? OpenDataPhilly (opendataphilly.org), SEPTA Routes & Stops Locations dataset, Spring 2025. - How many features does it contain? 22,478 - What CRS is it in? Did you need to transform it? WGS 84 / Pseudo-Mercator, I transformed it to match the ACS tract data using st_transform() --- 2. **Pose a research question** How equitably are SEPTA transit stops distributed across Philadelphia's census tracts, and do disparities in access vary by racial composition? --- 3. **Conduct spatial analysis** Use at least TWO spatial operations to answer your research question. **Required operations (choose 2+):** - Buffers - Spatial joins - Spatial filtering with predicates - Distance calculations - Intersections or unions - Point-in-polygon aggregation **Your Task:** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Your spatial analysis #Distance from each tract centroid to nearest transit stop philly_tracts_proj <- philly_tracts %>% st_transform(crs = 2272) septa_stops_proj <- septa_stops %>% st_transform(crs = 2272) #stops by census tract stops_by_tract <- st_join(septa_stops, philly_tracts) stops_count <- stops_by_tract %>% st_drop_geometry() %>% group_by(NAME) %>% summarise(num_stops = n()) stops_count ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 406 × 2 NAME num_stops 1 Census Tract 1.01; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 56 2 Census Tract 1.02; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 25 3 Census Tract 10.01; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 26 4 Census Tract 10.02; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 49 5 Census Tract 100; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 16 6 Census Tract 101; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 37 7 Census Tract 102; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 14 8 Census Tract 103; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 11 9 Census Tract 104; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 12 10 Census Tract 105; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania 44 # ℹ 396 more rows ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} #join back to main data philly_tracts_proj <- philly_tracts_proj %>% left_join(st_drop_geometry(stops_count), by = "NAME") %>% mutate(num_stops = replace_na(num_stops, 0), stops_per_1000 = round((num_stops / total_popE) * 1000, 2)) #make centroids tract_centroids <- philly_tracts_proj %>% st_centroid() dist_to_stop <- st_distance(tract_centroids, septa_stops_proj) tract_centroids$nearest_stop_miles <- round(apply(dist_to_stop, 1, min) / 5280, 2) #classify tracts by race philly_race_analysis <- tract_centroids %>% mutate( majority_race = case_when( whiteE / total_popE > 0.5 ~ "Majority White", blackE / total_popE > 0.5 ~ "Majority Black", asianE / total_popE > 0.5 ~ "Majority Asian", hispanicE / total_popE > 0.5 ~ "Majority Hispanic", TRUE ~ "No Majority" ) ) #summary statistics for race race_summary <- philly_race_analysis %>% st_drop_geometry() %>% group_by(majority_race) %>% summarise( num_tracts = n(), avg_stops = round(mean(num_stops, na.rm = TRUE), 2), avg_stops_per_1000 = round(mean(stops_per_1000, na.rm = TRUE), 2), avg_dist_nearest_stop = round(mean(nearest_stop_miles, na.rm = TRUE), 2) ) race_summary ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 5 majority_race num_tracts avg_stops avg_stops_per_1000 avg_dist_nearest_stop 1 Majority Black 145 32.1 25.2 0.09 2 Majority Hispan… 23 24.6 5.82 0.08 3 Majority White 149 40.7 14.8 0.11 4 No Majority 91 35.5 Inf 0.12 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} #map 1 ggplot() + geom_sf(data = philly_tracts_proj, aes(fill = num_stops), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.1) + scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Number of Stops per Census Tract", option = "plasma") + labs( title = "SEPTA Transit Stop Density by Census Tract", subtitle = "Philadelphia County" ) + theme_void() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lab_2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=672} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} #map2 philly_race_map <- philly_tracts_proj %>% mutate( majority_race = case_when( whiteE / total_popE > 0.5 ~ "Majority White", blackE / total_popE > 0.5 ~ "Majority Black", asianE / total_popE > 0.5 ~ "Majority Asian", hispanicE / total_popE > 0.5 ~ "Majority Hispanic", TRUE ~ "No Majority" ) ) septa_philly <- septa_stops_proj[philly_tracts_proj, ] ggplot() + geom_sf(data = philly_race_map, aes(fill = majority_race), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.1) + geom_sf(data = septa_philly, size = 0.1, alpha = 0.3) + labs( title = "SEPTA Stop Locations by Racial Composition", subtitle = "Points = Transit Stops", fill = "Majority Race" ) + theme_void() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lab_2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-2.png){width=672} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} #Chart 1 - distance by race ggplot(st_drop_geometry(philly_race_analysis), aes(x = majority_race, y = nearest_stop_miles, fill = majority_race)) + geom_boxplot() + labs( x = "Majority Race", y = "Distance to Nearest Stop (miles)", title = "Distance to Nearest SEPTA Stop by Racial Composition" ) + theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "none") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lab_2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-3.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Your interpretation:** SEPTA transit stops are unevenly distributed across Philadelphia's census tracts, with notable disparities along racial lines. Majority-Black and No Majority tracts tend to have higher raw stop counts but lower stops per capita, suggesting that while transit infrastructure is physically present, it may not be proportionally serving the population. Majority-White tracts, particularly in the northeast and northwest of the city, show greater average distances to the nearest transit stop, reflecting lower transit density in more car-dependent, suburban-style neighborhoods ---
